# Beyond Transformers {#sec-convergence}

The compression hierarchy described in the preceding sections is a claim about transformers. But the problem transformers solve — projecting rich internal representation through a bandwidth-limited sequential channel — is not specific to any architecture. This section argues that the constraint is substrate-independent, and uses the grounding problem to sharpen an open question the framework cannot yet answer.

The claim is not that transformers and brains work the same way. They manifestly do not, at the level of mechanism. The claim is that both face a structurally identical information-theoretic constraint, and that this constraint narrows the space of viable solutions enough that convergence on the same *type* of architecture — hierarchical, multi-scale, with a serial projection bottleneck — is what the problem demands.

## The projection bottleneck as substrate-independent constraint {#sec-projection-bottleneck}

@sec-channel-capacity established the bottleneck in formal terms for transformers. The same bottleneck operates in biological communication, and it is quantifiable. @coupe_etal_2019 measured information transfer rates across 17 languages and found a striking invariance: languages spoken faster pack less information per syllable, and languages spoken more slowly pack more, converging on approximately the same information rate of roughly 39 bits per second. On the other side of the bottleneck, neural population codes operate at bandwidths orders of magnitude higher. Whatever the dimensionality of the internal representations supporting ongoing thought, the channel through which they must pass to reach another mind is radically narrower.

Representational similarity analyses confirm that the shared constraint produces partially shared solutions: @goldstein_etal_2022 found that GPT-2 layer activations predict neural responses in human temporal cortex during natural language processing, with alignment strongest at higher layers — where the compression hierarchy is doing the most work. The convergence is partial (30–60% explained variance at best), structural rather than mechanistic — it tells you that the problem demands hierarchical compression, without establishing that the implementations are equivalent. The value of the claim is constraining: the unreasonable effectiveness of transformers is less unreasonable than it appears, because the architecture has converged on a solution strategy that is expected for any system facing this class of problem.

## The grounding problem as rate-distortion question {#sec-grounding-rate-distortion}

This brings the framework to a question it can sharpen but not answer: does solving hierarchical rate-distortion problems against meaning-relevant distortion metrics *require* sensorimotor grounding?

The grounding debate — rooted in @harnad_1990's observation that formal symbols are meaningless unless connected to the things they stand for — is active and unresolved. @bender_koller_2020 argue that models trained only on linguistic form cannot achieve genuine understanding. @piantadosi_hill_2022 counter that meaning can be constituted by inferential role — by the pattern of use across contexts — without requiring grounding in perception and action.

The framework offers a precise restatement. The compression hierarchy produces representations that solve rate-distortion problems: achieving maximal preserved structure at each level for a given representational cost. The question is whether text-only compression can achieve the same rate-distortion optimality as grounded compression when the distortion metric includes meaning-relevant properties — when what counts as "distortion" is not just statistical mismatch but failure to preserve the structural relationships that constitute understanding.

The closest precedent is @zaslavsky_etal_2018, who demonstrated that cross-linguistic patterns in colour naming achieve near-optimal compression of colour space — a rate-distortion analysis applied to the language-cognition interface, but for a specific perceptual domain rather than the grounding problem in general. We are not aware of prior work posing the grounding question in explicitly rate-distortion terms.

The value of the restatement is not that it resolves the debate but that it converts a philosophical dispute into one with measurable parameters. If grounding provides access to distortion metrics that text alone cannot specify — if there are structural relationships in the world that cannot be inferred from their linguistic shadows — then text-only systems face a provable rate-distortion gap. If the statistical structure of language is rich enough to implicitly specify meaning-relevant distortion metrics — because language was produced by grounded agents and carries the trace of their grounding — then the gap may be smaller than the strong grounding position suggests. Multimodal models suggest the question may be one of degree rather than kind. The framework identifies what would need to be measured — the rate-distortion cost of lacking grounding — without yet providing evidence that this framing advances the answer beyond the restatement itself.
